dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 4 for an overdose at a residence in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What led you to call 911 this morning?
We found my husband unresponsive in bed about five minutes ago.
Ma'am, do you know if he's breathing and responding?
No, I tried to wake him, but he wasn't moving. I think he's not breathing.
Okay, we'll check his eye response and airway. Can you step back for a second?
He opened his eyes slightly, but he didn't follow commands or respond verbally.
I'll apply stimulating pressure to his limbs. Any sign of movement?
Ma'am, can you tell us if he mentioned any symptoms before this?
He woke me up a few minutes ago and said, "I think I'm going to have a seizure." That's not like him.
Does he have any allergies?
No, he has no known drug allergies.
What medications is he taking regularly?
He takes Baclofen for muscle spasms and meloxicam for joint pain.
Any other medical issues?
He's borderline diabetic and had a slipped disc in his back.
When was the last time you saw him eat or drink?
He came home around 1-2 this morning and went to bed. That was the last time I saw him.
Was there anything strange he said or did after going to bed?
I wasn't with him after he went to bed. I just got out of bed when he started talking about a seizure.
Airway is clear and clearing of secretions at this point.
Respiratory rate is 22, unlabored and regular.
Chest rise and fall are even with normal bilateral breath sounds.
Heart rate is 118 and regular. Blood pressure is 132 over 83.
Pulse is 118, not weak or diminished.
SpO2 is at 98 percent.
Current EKG reads sinus tachycardia.
Glucose is 216 milligrams per deciliter.
Partner, I need help starting a peripheral IV on the extremity vein.
IV access achieved with successful 20g catheter on the arm. Normal saline at KVO running.
Time to apply cardiac monitoring in preparation for transport.
4 lead ECG monitoring initiated successfully with stable baseline readings.
Pulse has dropped to 94, still strong.
Breath sounds unchanged at 20 per minute.
Blood pressure now reads 137 over 101.
Glucose is now at 0 mg per dL after the IV started.
Patient is diaphoretic with normal skin tone and no obvious trauma or burns.
Pupils sluggish at 4 mm, not brisk. No signs of head or neck injury noted.
Breath sounds equal and clear bilaterally. Heart tones normal and regular.
Stable pelvis with no deformities or distress on the extremities observed.
No active verbal reporting of pain documented.
GCS 10 recorded: eye 4, verbal 4, motor 6. Incomplete neurologically due to altered mental status.
Capnography performed. Waveform consistent with current clinical condition.
Transporting to VCU Health Systems for further care and medical evaluation.
Patient is on stretcher, secured with restraints, ready to exit.
Poison control called, they agree with transport destination.
Two fire personnel riding with us to give support. I'll call the hospital with patient details now.
Secured to stretcher in semi-Fowler's position, all monitors running.
En route to the hospital, ETA approximately ten minutes.
Vital signs remain stable. Baseline established with no dilution of findings.
Capnography is normal, SpO2 stabilized at 98 percent.
Glucose at 0 mg/dL remains unchanged, no further intervention initiated.
No additional medications or procedures performed en route.
Hospital informed. Trauma bed 8 is set up, patient is prioritized.
56-year-old male with suspected overdose. GCS 10, medications disclosed on file.
Sinus tachy seen on 12-lead, 4 lead monitoring intact during scene and transport.
Room and team are ready for the patient. Transfer initiated once we arrive.
IV, ECG, capnography all active and functional for patient handoff.
Full report from ePCR given now, including medical and pharmacologic information.
You coordinated well with us. He's being evaluated for potential overdose.
I didn't expect to see a medical problem this early. Thanks for being prompt.
Hospital has received all relevant findings, including sinus tach on EKG.
We implemented semi-Fowler's position to improve oxygenation for transport.
Patient stable in extremities and abdominal exams, no signs of active trauma.
We'll file the report and restock for the next scene.
I appreciate your care. It helps knowing he's in a trauma center now.
Complete 12-lead EKG performed and documented in medical charting.
Timeline of events supported by ePCR including wife as key eyewitness.
Post-ingestion event with presumed Baclofen OD included in detailed hospital handoff.
No other known apps or conditions stated in history relayed in telemetry.
